---
title: "AI"
---

This page covers the Circle AI program, and it is important not to confuse its three tiers:

- **Proof-carrying architecture contracts** (the `AIT` and selected `AIRA` families, below) — real structural facts about transformer components: strided-attention coverage, RoPE relative position, RoPE position distinguishability under a declared discretized phase bank, and circulant equivariance. This is the substantive proved content, and it is what makes circle math earn a place here.
- **Finite-indexing bookkeeping** (the `AIA` / `AIM` / `AIRA` families) — elementary facts that an index stays in range and is unchanged by a full period, e.g. `memory_slot(bank, token) = token mod bank`. These are honest but **trivial**: they are instances of the S1 closure spine, infrastructure rather than evidence that circles help a model.
- **Exploratory fixtures** (the `-B` ids) — tiny synthetic benchmarks with ordinary baselines and controls; **not** model-quality claims, and several are illustrative-only (see below).

Local compute language is MLX/Mac-first.

<span class="showcase-backlink" data-showcase-ref="SHOW-010"></span>

The flagship standalone contract is the [RoPE position-distinguishability certifier](rope_certifier.qmd). It lets a non-Lean reader provide a rotary-position configuration and receive a theorem-linked exact discrete collision certificate plus a separate numerical real-phase margin report.

The exploratory fixtures (`AIA-B*`, `AIM-B*`, `AIRA-B*`, twenty-odd in all) follow one recipe: pit a circular feature against ordinary baselines — a scalar threshold, a learned-position lookup, a wrong-period table — plus a nonperiodic control where the circular feature *should not* win, with optional MLX scoring. That discipline (baselines and negative controls) is the good part. The honest caveat: many of these tasks are **tautological by construction** — the synthetic label is defined as a function of the phase, so a phase lookup wins by definition, demonstrating plumbing rather than usefulness. These fixtures are not model-quality claims and not performance claims.

<p data-showcase-ref="SHOW-010">Showcase evidence: <a href="../../showcase.qmd#SHOW-010">SHOW-010 AI memory, loop, and adapter schedules</a>.</p>

::: {.learning-unit}

## Case-Study Frame

The circular address spaces are phase channels, memory slots, adapter blocks, sparse-attention strides, and RoPE phase banks. The serious AI contribution is proof-carrying structure: exact coverage, collision, aliasing, and equivariance contracts that ordinary experiments often leave implicit. Performance stays a benchmark question.

:::

## What Circle Math Actually Proves Here

Most of this page is exploratory benchmark scaffolding. But two real transformer components
are finite-circle structures whose **structural** guarantees are now Lean-proved — not
relabeled modular arithmetic, and not model-quality claims, but exact facts about
*architecture* that practitioners actually reason about:

**Strided ("coil") attention coverage.** A strided head lets each token attend to
`i, i±k, i±2k, …` — a coil orbit on `C_n`. So the orbit/period theory *is* the
reachability theory:

- a stride-`k` head reaches exactly `n / gcd(n,k)` distinct positions (`AIT-T0001`);
- it reaches **every** position iff `gcd(n,k) = 1` (`AIT-T0002`, `AIT-T0003`);
- a local+coil hybrid reaches any dependency lag reached by either the local window or the admitted coil path (`AIT-T0010` through `AIT-T0014`);
- a finite family of admitted coil strides reaches the lags generated by any member stride, the local+family plan inherits both local and family reachability, a missed lag is exactly a miss by both the local window and all admitted stride-family paths, the local miss condition is exact for positive lags, full local-window coverage removes all positive-lag holes with an exact local-window threshold, adding one stride decomposes exactly against the previous family, the no-stride control reduces exactly to local-window reachability, increasing local-window or path-length budgets preserves existing lag coverage, complete-coverage status is equivalent to having no positive uncovered-lag witness, raw plus context-clipped candidate-budget upper bounds are explicit, the theorem-side lag-candidate list has exact in-context reachability semantics, reached lags map into query-indexed predecessor candidates, no-collision lists deduplicate without budget loss, structural conditions imply those no-collision predicates, a singleton no-wrap numeric condition proves duplicate-free lag candidates, head/tail residue disjointness composes multi-stride no-collision, a numeric no-wrap head/tail separation condition proves that disjointness premise, an ordered separated-family condition proves duplicate-free family residues, separated-family local-window conditions prove duplicate-free full lag candidates, separated-family query no-collision/exact raw-budget endpoints follow under predecessor injectivity, and the local-window-below-context condition proves that predecessor injectivity numerically (`AIT-T0015` through `AIT-T0075`).

That is a proved design rule for sparse/dilated attention: *pick strides coprime to the
context length and every token provably reaches every other; combine local and coil
paths when the task mixes near and long dependencies.*

The gap theorem is the contract form: it lets a tool report not only "these lags are reachable" but also "these lags are uncovered by this declared sparse plan." That is coverage bookkeeping, not attention quality.

**RoPE relative position.** Rotary embeddings encode position as rotation; their defining
property — interaction depends only on relative position — is the rotation law:

- shifting query and key by the same amount leaves the relative phase fixed (`AIT-T0004`);
- rotary rotations compose by adding strides (`AIT-T0005`).

**RoPE position distinguishability.** A discretized RoPE phase bank represents a position by residues modulo declared integer periods. This is not a claim that real RoPE has exact integer periods; it is the exact contract a certifier can check:

- one-channel collision is equivalent to the period dividing the ordered position gap (`AIRA-T0021`);
- one-channel distinguishability is equivalent to that period not dividing the gap (`AIRA-T0022`);
- if a context fits inside one declared period, that channel is injective on the context (`AIRA-T0023`);
- all-channel bank collision is equivalent to every declared period dividing the same gap (`AIRA-T0024`);
- bank distinguishability is equivalent to at least one declared period not dividing the gap (`AIRA-T0025`);
- if the bank contains a period at least the context length, every unequal pair in context is distinguished (`AIRA-T0026`).

**Circulant ("CoilLinear") token mixing.** A circulant mixer `(c ⋆ x)ᵢ = ∑ⱼ cⱼ·x(i−j)` is
the `C_n` group algebra — the math behind FNet / long-convolution mixing. Its structure is
proved:

- it **commutes with cyclic shift** — translation equivariance for the circulant mixer structure (`AIT-T0006`);
- circular convolution is commutative (`AIT-T0007`) and linear (`AIT-T0008`);
- the cyclic shift is a genuine group action (`AIT-T0009`).

<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIT-T0001"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIT-T0002"></div>

<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIT-T0010"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIT-T0011"></div>

<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIT-T0009"></div>

::: {.warning-box}
The honest split for AI: **Lean proves structure** (coverage, relative position,
equivariance, and algebraic mixer laws) — facts it genuinely can certify; **experiments prove
performance** (accuracy, efficiency, extrapolation) — which Lean cannot, and which remain
benchmark work, not proof claims. See [What "Proved" Means Here](../../what_proved_means.qmd).
:::

## A Runnable Block And An Honest Experiment

The proved components above are assembled into a small, runnable `CircleFormerBlock`
(RoPE encoding + coil sparse-attention + circulant mixing) in
`circle_math/applications/circle_transformer.py`. Its tests don't measure accuracy — they
verify the *proved properties hold in code*: circulant shift-equivariance to machine
precision, RoPE scores depending only on relative position, and strided-head coverage
matching `gcd(n,k)` exactly.

For the performance question — which only an experiment can answer — there is a controlled,
**honestly-reported** bias–variance study
(`circle_math/applications/circle_transformer_experiment.py`): a circulant mixer (`n`
parameters by construction, shift-structured bias) versus a dense mixer (`n²` parameters, no bias), both
fit by exact least squares, on a shift-structured target and on an unstructured control.
The measured held-out error:

```text
target          n_train   circulant      dense     winner
shift-struct         12     0.0003       3.2636   circulant
shift-struct         24     0.0001       0.0064   circulant
unstructured         24     1.0156       0.0041   dense
unstructured        200     0.9784       0.0002   dense
```

The honest reading — including where circle structure **loses**: when the dependency is
shift-structured, the circulant model generalizes far better from few samples (right bias,
fewer parameters); when it is not, the circulant model hits an *irreducible error floor* —
it structurally cannot represent a non-circulant map — and the dense model wins. The
inductive bias helps **iff** the assumption holds. This is a controlled linear study, not a
claim about deep transformers; it is exactly the kind of result the AI track should report
straight, wins and losses alike.

## AI Program Map

AI is now a dedicated Circle Calculus program, not a side note. The avenues to explore are:

- phase channels and periodic feature routing,
- cyclic memory slots, alias diagnostics, and retrieval fixtures,
- coil/sparse attention for long-context path selection,
- looped and recursive transformer schedules with loop phase, exit certificates, and overthinking guardrails,
- adapter blocks, CoilLinear/circulant mixers, CoilRA, and block-cyclic parameter sharing,
- RoPE, MultiCoil RoPE, winding-aware positions, and torus-valued positional views,
- recurrent, state-space, and convolutional sequence models with explicit period structure,
- harmonic, Fourier, circulant, and NTT-adjacent features,
- quaternion, spherical, Hopf/fiber, and rotation-aware representations, and
- proof-carrying model components whose indexing or schedule behavior can be checked in Lean.

Every avenue needs ordinary baselines, negative controls, and reproducible scripts before it becomes a claim about model quality or speed.

## Optional Downstream Transfer Lane

Circle AI should stand on its own first: public theorem ids, executable fixtures, ordinary baselines, negative controls, and Living Book explanations must be useful without any private project. A local Theseus-Hive transfer lane exists as optional downstream context, but it is not the source of truth for Circle AI claims.

The transfer boundary is still useful as a pattern: downstream systems should consume explicit contracts, not loose inspiration.

```text
Circle AI proofs and fixtures
  -> recurrence / routing / memory / phase / mixer contracts
  -> private Theseus-Hive experiments
  -> ordinary baselines and negative controls
  -> only then usefulness claims
```

The transfer plan lives in [`docs/THESEUS_HIVE_AI_TRANSFER.md`](https://github.com/corbensorenson/circle-calculus/blob/main/docs/THESEUS_HIVE_AI_TRANSFER.md). It proposes recurrence contracts, stride/fanout coverage contracts, cyclic memory with residue plus winding, MultiCoil feature tags, circulant/block-cyclic mixer baselines, and seed-rule provenance experiments. These should be tested against Theseus-Hive's existing position buckets, candidate fanout, work-budget admission, score-based context retention, dense/low-rank mixers, and object-only artifact storage.

The first concrete artifact is generated by:

```bash
make theseus-ai-contracts
```

It writes [`site/data/generated/theseus_hive_ai_contracts.json`](https://github.com/corbensorenson/circle-calculus/blob/main/site/data/generated/theseus_hive_ai_contracts.json), with deterministic recurrence, fanout, memory, phase-feature, mixer, and seed-rule contract fixtures.

The companion Theseus-Hive workspace now has six report-only local layers:

- `scripts/circle_ai_contract_consumer.py` reads this pack and emits `reports/circle_ai_contract_consumer.json` and `.md`;
- `scripts/circle_ai_private_workload_smoke.py` gives each family a named structural workload slot;
- `scripts/circle_ai_private_proxy_benchmark.py` measures deterministic baseline metrics for those six proxy workloads without training or scoring a learned model;
- `scripts/circle_ai_real_workload_attachments.py` reads local Theseus-Hive artifacts and emits aggregate-only workload attachments without exporting private row content;
- `scripts/circle_ai_feedback_summary.py` exports report presence, trigger states, booleans, counts, and numeric metrics as an optional public-safe feedback handoff;
- `scripts/circle_ai_scored_private_benchmarks.py` compares candidate-fanout, recurrence work-budget, cyclic-memory, phase-feature, route/ranker mixer, router-head attachment, and seed-rule contracts against ordinary baselines using private candidate-manifest score/gate fields, local private heldout tests, the local context-packet ledger, private sequence rows, local Octopus router traces, current router-head decisions, and safe provenance envelopes.

When the generated contract pack is available, the private compiled ratchet now runs the consumer, structural smoke, deterministic proxy, aggregate real-workload attachment, lane-integrity audit, and feedback-summary layers automatically. The scored private layer remains data-gated: it should only run when the required private candidate, router, context, and provenance artifacts are present. The companion `scripts/circle_ai_lane_integrity_audit.py` report verifies that compiled/data-gated boundary as wiring evidence only. The Living Book can import only the sanitized feedback summary through `make theseus-ai-feedback`; raw private reports do not belong in this public repository.

The current imported feedback state is GREEN for handoff safety: five required safe reports are present, four are YELLOW evidence layers, the lane-integrity audit is GREEN, and the scored private benchmark remains missing/data-gated. The import records no private export, no external inference, no training mutation, and no promotion evidence. That makes the loop auditable; it does not make a model-quality claim.

The smoke workload ids are:

```text
circle_recurrence_budget_trace_smoke
circle_strided_candidate_coverage_smoke
circle_memory_alias_visibility_smoke
circle_phase_feature_invariance_smoke
circle_mixer_parameter_accounting_smoke
circle_seed_rule_regeneration_smoke
```

The proxy benchmark ids are:

```text
circle_recurrence_budget_proxy_v1
circle_candidate_fanout_proxy_v1
circle_memory_alias_proxy_v1
circle_phase_feature_proxy_v1
circle_mixer_parameter_proxy_v1
circle_seed_rule_regeneration_proxy_v1
```

The real aggregate attachment ids are:

```text
circle_recurrence_budget_real_rows_v1
circle_candidate_fanout_real_manifest_v1
circle_memory_alias_real_trace_v1
circle_phase_feature_real_sequence_v1
circle_mixer_real_report_summary_v1
circle_seed_rule_real_provenance_summary_v1
```

The scored private benchmark ids are:

```text
circle_candidate_fanout_scored_manifest_v1
circle_candidate_fanout_semantic_frontier_v1
circle_candidate_fanout_equal_budget_semantic_v1
circle_recurrence_work_budget_semantic_v1
circle_memory_context_packet_retrieval_v1
circle_phase_feature_sequence_ablation_v1
circle_mixer_route_ranker_ablation_v1
circle_mixer_router_head_attachment_v1
circle_seed_rule_exact_regeneration_v1
circle_seed_rule_source_change_stress_v1
circle_seed_rule_workflow_tool_card_rebuild_v1
circle_seed_rule_update_cycle_effort_v1
```

The first compares Circle striding with sequential, round-robin, local-window, deterministic-random-like, and mode-stratified existing selection on private candidate-manifest scores and gates. The second joins existing private frontier score reports to candidate manifests and reports semantic pass-rate context. The third freshly executes equal-budget candidate selections against local private heldout tests and exports aggregate-only pass-rate/runtime counters. The fourth treats the exported recurrence contract as a deterministic work-budget schedule and compares it with ordinary fixed-depth baselines under the same local private tests. The fifth scores context-packet identity and retention policies without exporting packet text. The sixth scores phase-feature ablations without exporting labels or row content. The seventh scores route/ranker mixer transforms without exporting task text, feature names, label names, or model weights. The eighth attaches those mixer policies to current Octopus router-head holdout decisions and exports only aggregate decision agreement and accuracy. The ninth scores seed-rule exact regeneration over safe provenance envelopes without exporting artifact bodies, packet text, command text, row content, code, tests, model weights, or private payloads. The tenth stress-tests seed-rule provenance under source-change, edit-cost, verifier-residual, and downstream-reuse scenarios over safe hashed envelopes. The eleventh rebuilds safe workflow/tool-card envelopes from hashed workflow compiler, registry, hook, routing, update, artifact, and benchmark-card sources. The twelfth instruments safe update-cycle ledgers for runtime, human-edit-minute, review-step, and maintenance-mode field availability.

The semantic execution run is useful but not a win claim: on the full 1040-task private frontier across 26 categories, Circle stride budget-one, sequential rank-one, Circle stride budget-two, and sequential rank-two all report pass rate `1.0`; Circle best minus ordinary best is `0.0`. Direct projection of the exported global path onto per-task candidate ranks still performs badly in the score-context layer. The next benchmark needs a Circle selection rule that differs from ordinary rank-one when evidence supports it.

The recurrence work-budget run is also useful but not a win claim: on the same full frontier, Circle repeating-budget and ordinary rank-one both report pass rate `1.0`; Circle best minus ordinary best is `0.0`; Circle requested budget total is `2730` versus `1040` for ordinary rank-one. It proves the scored recurrence bridge exists, but the current task is saturated by the simplest ordinary baseline.

The memory run is useful but also not a win claim: on the local context-packet ledger, full residue-plus-winding identity hits all `37` packet targets at retention cost `74`, while the equal-budget Circle slot-balanced policy and ordinary score-top/FIFO/slot-only baselines all hit `0.216216` of targets under budget `8`. It proves the scored memory bridge exists, but it does not show downstream retrieval quality or latency advantage.

The phase-feature run is useful but also not a win claim: on `9750` private sequence/category rows with an ordered `7800`/`1950` train/eval split and `339` aggregate categories, Circle contract periods `[5, 7]`, Circle relative period `5`, wrong periods `[6, 8]`, and no-phase majority all report accuracy `0.528205`. Circle minus wrong-period, Circle minus no-phase, and Circle minus best ordinary are all `0.0`. It proves the scored phase bridge exists, but it does not show route/ranker or downstream model usefulness.

The route/ranker mixer run is useful but still not a deployed-router claim: on `94` local Octopus router trace examples with `80` train and `14` eval examples, no-mixer exact-set accuracy is `0.0`, dense hashed is `1.0`, low-rank hashed is `0.714286`, Circle circulant is `1.0`, and Circle block-cyclic is `1.0`. The best ordinary policy uses `2048` counted parameters; the best Circle policy uses `8`, so the Circle parameter ratio is `0.003906` at tied heldout trace accuracy. This is parameter-efficiency evidence for this fixture only, not runtime, memory, or production route quality.

The router-head attachment run moves the same mixer candidates one step closer to the active route path while still staying aggregate-only: on `14` current Octopus holdout decisions, no-mixer agreement is `0.0`, dense hashed is `1.0`, low-rank hashed is `0.714286`, Circle circulant is `1.0`, and Circle block-cyclic is `1.0`. The current router head also reports exact-set accuracy `1.0`; the best Circle policy uses `8` counted parameters versus `2048` for the best ordinary dense policy, with Circle minus current router-head accuracy `0.0`. This is decision-attachment evidence, not actual training, runtime, memory, or model-quality evidence.

The actual Octopus router-head trainer now also has opt-in pure and residual Circle mixer shadow paths: `scripts/train_octopus_router_head.py --circle-mixer circulant`, `--circle-mixer block_cyclic`, `--circle-mixer circulant_residual`, or `--circle-mixer block_cyclic_residual`. It leaves the default no-mixer trainer unchanged. The pure modes replace sparse router-head features with finite cyclic channels; the residual modes keep the raw features and add cyclic channels beside them. The private-safe aggregate benchmark `circle_router_head_mixer_shadow_training_v1` runs baseline, pure Circle, and residual Circle modes in memory and exports only aggregate metrics plus hashed failure summaries. A Mac shadow run over the current local ORA report, arm registry, and workflow routing traces scored `14` holdout cases: all five modes reported exact-set accuracy `1.0`, risk-routing accuracy `1.0`, arm micro-F1 `1.0`, exact mismatch count `0`, and zero external inference calls. Each Circle trainer mode used `4` shared kernel parameters. A `5`-repetition run recorded stable aggregate metrics. Validation-run median elapsed time was `10.704` ms for baseline, `112.769` ms for pure circulant, `96.332` ms for pure block-cyclic, `100.91` ms for residual circulant, and `97.362` ms for residual block-cyclic; median Python `tracemalloc` peak bytes were `117078`, `335297`, `339165`, `447689`, and `438394`, respectively, so runtime and memory remain unclaimed. The keyword-boundary contrastive diagnostic adds `45` train and `45` holdout examples: the no-mixer baseline scores exact accuracy `1.0`, pure circulant and pure block-cyclic score `0.6667`, and full-gain residual circulant/residual block-cyclic improve to `0.8667`/`0.8222` while still trailing baseline. A quality-only residual gain sweep finds gains `0.05`, `0.1`, `0.25`, and `0.5` preserve baseline exactness on this synthetic diagnostic for both residual modes, while full gain `1.0` regresses. The real-trace boundary diagnostic re-splits `30` private workflow routing rows into `71` train and `9` holdout examples across `5` eligible private labelsets; full-gain Circle modes score `0.8889` exact accuracy against the no-mixer `1.0`, while residual gains `0.05`, `0.1`, and `0.25` preserve baseline exactness and `0.5`/`1.0` regress. The coverage report sees `31` compatible real rows, `12` hashed labelset bins, `7` singleton bins, `11` under-target bins, and a `40`-row gap to bring every bin to the target of `5` rows; the private trace collection planner turns those hashed gaps into bounded row budgets and validation commands without exporting task text, arm labels, feature names, model weights, private payloads, or promotion evidence. Future autonomous-goal traces now carry router-head-compatible `workflow`, safe `command`, `routing_pattern`, `expected_arms`, `split`, and `success` fields, checked by a private-side producer self-test; the producer also has scratch output arguments and a `head_router` fallback so a strict non-live smoke audit can produce one current-schema compatible row with no external inference, private export, or promotion evidence. The compiled private ratchet now runs schema audit, boundary coverage, collection planning, and a static trace-lane integrity audit after arm-registry generation and before router-head training consumes workflow or autonomous routing-memory traces. The integrity audit checks compiled command order, output-map discoverability, workflow classifiers, and two-source trainer/tool-card wiring without reading private trace payloads. This is compatibility, regression, tuning, trace-quality, coverage, and wiring evidence inside the real trainer path, not evidence of better routing, runtime, memory, transfer, or promotion readiness.

The seed-rule exact-regeneration run is useful but still not a general compression or AI claim: it reads `3` local provenance/tool-memory source reports and regenerates `75` safe envelope records across `6` record kinds. Object-only storage and Circle seed-rule-with-verifier both report exact regeneration `1.0`; Circle verifier residual count is `0`; template and freeform controls both report exact regeneration `0.0`. The Circle recipe length is `527` bytes versus `13291` bytes for object-only storage, a ratio of `0.039651`. This is source-dependent provenance evidence only.

The seed-rule source-change stress run is useful but still not a general compression or AI claim: it applies `4` deterministic source-change scenarios and scores `20` downstream reuse queries over the safe envelope records. Object-only storage and Circle seed-rule-with-verifier both report exact regeneration `1.0` and downstream reuse hit rate `1.0`; Circle verifier residual count is `0`; template and freeform controls both report exact regeneration `0.0`. Circle average description length is `590.0` versus object-only `10344.75`, and Circle average edit cost is `463.75` versus object-only `6327.5`. This is source-change provenance tooling evidence only.

The workflow/tool-card rebuild run is useful but still not a human-time or AI claim: it reads `72` safe sources, including `64` benchmark-card sources, and rebuilds `188` safe envelope records across `13` record kinds. Object-only storage and Circle seed-rule-with-verifier both report exact rebuild `1.0` and downstream reuse hit rate `1.0`; Circle verifier residual count is `0`; inventory and registry-only baselines both report exact rebuild `0.0`. Circle automated rebuild work units are `9334` versus object-only `59730`. Human edit time is not measured in this fixture.

The update-cycle effort run is useful as instrumentation but still not a maintenance-savings or AI claim: it reads `13` safe sources and `289` aggregate event records. It finds `212` runtime fields with total runtime `92252.0` ms, average runtime `435.151` ms, and p95 runtime `864.0` ms. It now finds `3` `review_step_count` fields with total review steps `15.0`, plus `2` explicit `maintenance_mode` fields: one `object_only` row and one `circle_seed_rule_rebuild` row. It still finds `0` `human_edit_minutes` fields. The private Theseus-Hive side now has `scripts/hive_record_maintenance_effort.py` for measured human-edit rows, but no measured rows are present in the current aggregate run. The next step is measured human-edit time on paired object-only versus Circle maintenance rows, not a win claim.

All current reports are `YELLOW`: the bridge loads all six contract families, the smoke workloads pass, deterministic proxy metrics exist, six local aggregate workload attachments exist, the scored candidate-manifest benchmark exists, the semantic score-context layer exists, the full-frontier semantic execution layer exists, the recurrence work-budget execution layer exists, the memory context-packet retrieval layer exists, the phase-feature ablation layer exists, the route/ranker mixer ablation layer exists, the router-head mixer attachment layer exists, the seed-rule exact-regeneration layer exists, the seed-rule source-change stress layer exists, the workflow/tool-card rebuild layer exists, and update-cycle effort instrumentation exists, but there are no learned-model quality metrics or Circle advantage results yet.

::: {.warning-box}
This lane is not evidence that Circle Calculus has improved any AI system. It is an optional experiment plan. Lean can certify finite schedule, index, coverage, relative-phase, and equivariance facts; public standalone benchmarks must carry public claims, and any private downstream benchmark remains separate evidence.
:::

## Phase-Channel Baseline Path

The first AI fixture asks the smallest useful question: if labels are actually periodic, can a phase-channel lookup recover them, and do ordinary controls stay visible? The same widget also includes a nonperiodic scalar-threshold task where the circular feature should not be treated as helpful.

<div class="widget-panel" data-widget="phase_channel_baseline" data-theorem-id="AIA-T0001" data-dictionary-ids="COMMON-0026 COMMON-0027"></div>

## Learned-Feature Baseline Path

The next AI fixture keeps the feature tiny but adds ordinary learned controls. A correct cyclic phase table is compared against a dense scalar threshold, an absolute-position lookup, and a wrong-period phase table. The nonperiodic control keeps the scalar threshold visible as the right baseline when the target is not periodic.

<div class="widget-panel" data-widget="learned_feature_baseline" data-theorem-id="AIA-T0001" data-dictionary-ids="COMMON-0026 COMMON-0027"></div>

## Harmonic/Fourier Feature Path

The harmonic feature fixture asks the same known-period question with a sine/cosine encoding instead of an integer phase key. The correct period should match the phase lookup, while a wrong-frequency feature, scalar threshold, and absolute-position lookup remain visible controls.

This is the safe first step toward RoPE-adjacent thinking: it shows the finite phase arithmetic behind a Fourier-style feature, not a language-model result.

<div class="widget-panel" data-widget="harmonic_feature_baseline" data-theorem-id="AIA-T0001" data-dictionary-ids="COMMON-0026 COMMON-0027 COMMON-0046"></div>

## Backend Parity Path

Backend parity asks a different question from model quality: do the deterministic fixture scores agree when the same predictions are scored on CPU and optional MLX? The static widget shows the fixed CPU cases used by `AIA-B0003`; the local Python fixture is still the source for MLX availability and MLX-vs-CPU delta.

<div class="widget-panel" data-widget="backend_parity_fixture" data-dictionary-ids="COMMON-0026 COMMON-0028 COMMON-0030 COMMON-0046 COMMON-0047 COMMON-0051 COMMON-0052"></div>

## Cyclic Memory Slot Path

Cyclic memory starts with a plain finite address: `memory_slot(bank_size, token) = token mod bank_size`. The useful interface question is not whether this improves a model by itself, but whether slot assignment, alias collisions, and closure after full bank passes are visible before running a benchmark.

The widget below shows that finite layer. It is not a retrieval-quality result and not a memory-scaling claim; it is only the indexing and collision bookkeeping used by the current Lean/Python artifacts.

<div class="widget-panel" data-widget="cyclic_memory_slots" data-theorem-id="AIM-T0001" data-dictionary-ids="COMMON-0028 COMMON-0029"></div>

The second memory contract is a KV-cache ring-buffer window certificate. Given `cache_size`, `current`, and `token`, the sidecar reports the token slot, current slot, lag from current, whether the token is retained, whether a retained older token is distinct from the current slot, and whether the next same-slot overwrite occurs after the current read point. The sidecar also checks ordered, unordered, and batch slot distinctness for retained tokens in the same live window. The Lean theorems `AIM-T0059` through `AIM-T0068` certify the finite slot and overwrite-window facts; the certificate is not a paging policy, deployment-safety proof, throughput result, memory-saving result, or retrieval-quality claim.

```bash
python sidecars/PAPER_AI_02_COIL_ATTENTION_AND_MEMORY/python/benchmark_kv_cache_ring_buffer.py
```

<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIM-T0059"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIM-T0060"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIM-T0061"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIM-T0062"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIM-T0063"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIM-T0064"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIM-T0065"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIM-T0066"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIM-T0067"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIM-T0068"></div>
<div class="dictionary-box" data-dictionary-id="COMMON-0081"></div>

## Coil Retrieval Reachability Path

Coil retrieval asks a narrower question than "does this improve attention?": can a fixed finite stride path reach a known long-lag dependency that a local window misses, while ordinary local attention still wins on a near-lag control?

`AIM-B0002` is the current executable fixture for that question. The widget below exposes the candidate-set arithmetic behind it: selected coil path, local window, wrong stride, full-attention oracle, and near-lag controls. It is not a retrieval-quality claim and not a replacement for learned attention baselines.

<div class="widget-panel" data-widget="coil_retrieval_reachability" data-dictionary-ids="COMMON-0047 COMMON-0028 COMMON-0029"></div>

## Content-Gated Coil/Local Path

The next retrieval question is routing: if a query needs a long dependency, use the coil path; if it needs a near dependency, use the local window. The current `AIM-B0004` fixture is hand-coded so the route is visible and auditable before any learned gate is introduced.

The widget below compares content-gated routing against static coil, static local, wrong-gate, union-candidate, and full-attention oracle baselines. Candidate budget is shown beside hit rate so a full oracle does not look cheaper than it is.

<div class="widget-panel" data-widget="content_gated_retrieval" data-dictionary-ids="COMMON-0057 COMMON-0047 COMMON-0028"></div>

The learned version fits a tiny phase-to-route lookup from training examples, then compares it with static coil, static local, wrong-period, flipped-gate, union-candidate, and full-attention controls. It is a route-table fixture, not a learned-model quality claim.

<div class="widget-panel" data-widget="learned_content_gate_retrieval" data-dictionary-ids="COMMON-0057 COMMON-0047 COMMON-0028"></div>

## Hybrid Sparse-Attention Path

The next standalone sparse-attention step is not Theseus-specific: combine local and coil candidates, then ask whether the combined sparse set covers the dependencies it was designed for while staying cheaper than full attention.

`AIM-B0016` is the current executable fixture. It mixes near dependencies with long dependencies that lie on the selected coil path. The hybrid local+coil candidate set reaches all structured targets with average candidate count `9`, while the full-attention oracle uses `96` candidates. Local-only, coil-only, and wrong-stride controls miss part of the structured task. The nonstructured control is just as important: arbitrary lags are not covered by the sparse pattern, so full attention remains the oracle.

`AIM-B0017` extends that idea from one coil stride to a finite stride family. This is the first multi-head sparse-attention coverage lesson: a family of admitted strides can cover a wider set of generated lags than a single stride while keeping candidate count far below full attention. It now emits an explicit coverage certificate too: for the default `C_120` plan with local window `4`, path length `3`, and strides `(7,13)`, only positive lags `1,2,3,4,7,14,21,13,26,39` are covered. The other `109` positive lags are gap certificates under `AIT-T0020`, with the concrete no-local/no-stride-step condition stated by `AIT-T0021`; `AIT-T0028` proves the local miss condition is exactly `window < lag` for positive lags, `AIT-T0022` records the dense-local complete-coverage limit, `AIT-T0023` proves the local-only threshold is exact, `AIT-T0024`/`AIT-T0025` prove the exact recursion for adding one stride to the family, `AIT-T0026`/`AIT-T0027` prove the empty-family control reduces to local reachability, and `AIT-T0029` through `AIT-T0032` prove that conservative increases to local-window or path-length budgets preserve already covered lags. `AIT-T0033` through `AIT-T0035` package the certificate status: complete coverage is exactly no positive uncovered-lag witness, dense local coverage implies complete coverage, and conservative budget increases preserve complete coverage. `AIT-T0036` through `AIT-T0038` make raw candidate-budget accounting explicit, `AIT-T0039` through `AIT-T0042` add the tighter context-clipped deduplicated-budget cap `min(context, raw_budget)`, `AIT-T0043` through `AIT-T0050` prove the theorem-side lag-candidate list has exact in-context reachability semantics, `AIT-T0051` through `AIT-T0054` map reached lags into query-indexed predecessor candidates, `AIT-T0055`/`AIT-T0056` prove no-collision lists deduplicate without budget loss, `AIT-T0057`/`AIT-T0058` derive those no-collision predicates from structural checks, `AIT-T0059` through `AIT-T0062` prove the first singleton no-wrap numeric sufficient condition, `AIT-T0063` makes residue no-collision compositional across a finite stride family, `AIT-T0064` proves a numeric no-wrap separation condition for the head/tail disjointness premise, `AIT-T0065` proves duplicate-free residues for an ordered no-wrap separated stride family, `AIT-T0066`/`AIT-T0067` lift separated families whose strides start beyond the local window to duplicate-free theorem-side lag candidates, `AIT-T0068` through `AIT-T0070` package query no-collision plus exact raw-budget endpoints under predecessor injectivity, and `AIT-T0071` through `AIT-T0075` prove the numeric `window < n` condition that supplies predecessor injectivity for the generated lag candidates. The wrong-family and nonstructured controls keep the claim honest.

<div class="widget-panel" data-widget="stride_family_attention" data-theorem-id="AIT-T0021" data-dictionary-ids="COMMON-0075 COMMON-0079 COMMON-0047 COMMON-0029"></div>

This is the right kind of Circle AI evidence: useful when the dependency structure matches the circle/coil assumption, visibly wrong when it does not. It is candidate-set reachability and budget accounting only, not a neural attention-quality, runtime, memory, or model-quality claim.

## Adapter Parameter-Budget Path

CoilRA-style adapter work needs ordinary accounting before it can make any efficiency claim: how many parameters does a dense adapter use, how many does a LoRA-style count use, and how much alias pressure is introduced when channels share a block-cyclic table?

`AIRA-B0004` is the current executable fixture for that boundary. The widget below compares dense per-channel, LoRA-style low-rank, and block-cyclic shared-table counts, then shows how channels load into adapter blocks. It is not evidence about fine-tuning quality, runtime, memory, training stability, or hardware efficiency.

<div class="widget-panel" data-widget="adapter_parameter_budget" data-dictionary-ids="COMMON-0056 COMMON-0030 COMMON-0031"></div>

## Circulant Mixer Validation Path

CoilLinear/circulant-mixer work starts with a basic validation question: does the circular convolution path produce exactly the same output as the dense circulant matrix it claims to represent?

`AIRA-B0005` is the current executable fixture for that boundary. The widget below compares circular convolution with the dense circulant-matrix product, shows a wrong-shift control, and reports dense-vs-circulant parameter counts. It is not a neural-layer quality, runtime, memory, training-stability, or hardware-efficiency result.

<div class="widget-panel" data-widget="circulant_mixer_validation" data-dictionary-ids="COMMON-0058 COMMON-0056 COMMON-0046"></div>

## Block-Cyclic Mixer Validation Path

Block-cyclic mixer work asks the next finite-addressing question: when a dense matrix cell `(row, column)` is represented by the shared cell `(row mod block_size, column mod block_size)`, do the row and column residue rules close exactly as advertised?

The proved facts `AIRA-T0011` through `AIRA-T0015` certify the row/column cell bounds and closure under whole block-size passes. `AIRA-B0007` is the executable fixture: it compares the block-cyclic matrix-vector path against the dense matrix it represents, records a wrong-row-shift control, and reports parameter/cell-alias diagnostics. It is not a neural-layer quality, runtime, memory, training-stability, or hardware-efficiency result.

<div class="widget-panel" data-widget="block_cyclic_mixer_validation" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0013" data-dictionary-ids="COMMON-0073 COMMON-0058 COMMON-0056"></div>

## MultiCoil Positional Phase Path

MultiCoil/RoPE-style work begins with a modest bookkeeping question: if a position has several phase periods, what is its combined phase tuple and when does that tuple repeat?

`AIRA-B0002` is the executable fixture for positional phase labels. `AIRA-T0016` through `AIRA-T0020` add the first formal two-period closure spine: each phase coordinate is bounded by its period, position zero maps to the zero pair, and adding `period_a * period_b` or any whole number of product-cycle passes preserves the pair. `AIRA-B0008` is the matching closure fixture; it compares the proof-backed product cycle, the ordinary lcm cycle, and a wrong-shift control.

The widget below shows the combined phase tuple, product-cycle closure, lcm comparison, and wrong-shift mismatch. It is not evidence that MultiCoil improves RoPE, attention, or language models.

<div class="widget-panel" data-widget="multicoil_phase_explorer" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0019" data-dictionary-ids="COMMON-0074 COMMON-0046 COMMON-0026"></div>

## RoPE-Style Relative Phase Path

RoPE-style relative phase asks a related but different question: what phase feature is induced by the lag between a query position and a key position?

`AIRA-B0003` is the current executable fixture for this idea. The widget below shows the correct-period relative feature, a wrong-period control, and the closure check after shifting query or key by a full period. It is not a standard RoPE benchmark or model-quality result.

<div class="widget-panel" data-widget="rope_relative_phase" data-dictionary-ids="COMMON-0051 COMMON-0050 COMMON-0026"></div>

## RoPE Position-Distinguishability Certifier

The standalone RoPE contract asks a sharper engineering question: under a declared discretized phase-bank policy, do two positions collide in every channel, or does at least one channel distinguish them?

`AIRA-T0024` is the main proof-carrying contract:

```text
all-channel collision
iff
every declared period divides the position gap
```

`AIRA-B0009` is the public certifier surface. It reports exact discrete pass/fail with theorem ids, then separately reports a numerical real-phase margin. The margin is useful, but it is not a Lean proof and not a context-length or model-quality claim.

[Open the guided RoPE certifier lesson](rope_certifier.qmd).

## Winding-Aware Position Path

Residue-only phase answers where a position lands on the finite circle. It does not answer how many full passes have already happened. That matters for long-position bookkeeping: positions with the same residue can carry different labels, memories, or recurrence histories.

The proved facts `AIRA-T0006` through `AIRA-T0010` make the finite split explicit:

```text
position = winding * period + residue
```

`AIRA-B0006` is the executable fixture for this boundary. It compares residue-plus-winding features against residue-only, wrong-period winding, learned absolute-position, and scalar-threshold baselines. The nonperiodic control is deliberately scalar, so the ordinary threshold baseline wins there. This is not a RoPE improvement claim, a context-length claim, or a language-model result.

<div class="widget-panel" data-widget="winding_aware_position" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0009" data-dictionary-ids="COMMON-0072 COMMON-0026 COMMON-0046"></div>

## Looped Recurrence Path

Looped and recursive transformers reuse model blocks across multiple computation passes. The Circle Calculus version should make each pass explicit: loop phase, recurrence budget, state provenance, exit decision, and overthinking boundary. That is a possible interface contribution, not a proved model improvement.

The benchmark lane is `P5-EDGE-010`, seeded by `AIM-B0003`, `AIM-B0011`, `AIM-B0005`, `AIM-B0012`, `AIM-B0006`, `AIM-B0007`, `AIM-B0013`, `AIM-B0008`, `AIM-B0014`, `AIM-B0009`, and `AIM-B0015`, with `AIM-B0010` covering the adjacent learned coil/local route-gating story. It should expand toward ordinary dense transformer depth, Universal Transformer recurrence, fixed looped transformers, adaptive early-exit models, recurrent-memory transformers, token-level Mixture-of-Recursions, sparse/MoE looped models, learned attention gates, full/sparse attention baselines, RWKV/Mamba-style recurrent or state-space models, and nonrecursive transformer baselines.

The first lesson goal is to make recurrence readable:

```text
shared block
  -> loop phase
  -> active token set / resolution level
  -> state update
  -> exit or continue
  -> score trace
  -> overthinking boundary
```

The proof boundary stays strict: Lean can certify finite schedule and indexing facts; Python or MLX benchmarks must carry model-quality, runtime, memory, and parameter claims.

The widget below is the smallest interactive version of that boundary. It computes the same finite schedule quantities used by the current Lean/Python layer: phase, required loop depth, token recurrence budget, capped training-free budget, exit availability, overthinking boundary, one-period closure, and whole-loop-pass closure. It is not a proof and it is not evidence of model quality.

<div class="widget-panel" data-widget="loop_recurrence_budget" data-theorem-id="AIM-T0018" data-dictionary-ids="COMMON-0052 COMMON-0053 COMMON-0054 COMMON-0059 COMMON-0067"></div>

`AIM-B0011` turns the exit decision into a small certificate: required depth, score trace, first exit step, budget status, and guardrail status. Lean now proves that a certificate implies exit availability, that the training-free wrapper budget selects the certified exit step, and that certified exit steps and guardrail boundaries stay unchanged after whole loop-period passes. The control keeps the same finite logic but uses a deliberately insufficient fixed budget, so the no-exit case is visible.

<div class="widget-panel" data-widget="loop_exit_certificate" data-theorem-id="AIM-T0033" data-dictionary-ids="COMMON-0052 COMMON-0053 COMMON-0054 COMMON-0059 COMMON-0067"></div>

`AIM-B0005` turns that lesson into token-level bookkeeping:

```text
token
  -> routed loop budget
  -> selected middle block
  -> active or halted at each loop step
  -> coarse/fine resolution label
  -> wrong-loop and over-loop controls
```

This is the Circle Calculus contribution to the interface: recurrence provenance should be visible before it is sold as reasoning or throughput.

Lean now pins down the active-step boundary used by that fixture: every token is active at step one, active-step membership is invariant under whole loop-period token shifts, active steps stay inside the positive loop period, and steps beyond that period are inactive. The widget below exposes the deterministic `AIM-B0005` fixture: each token gets a finite recurrence budget, loop steps show how many tokens remain active, resolution labels alternate by step, and fixed/wrong/over-loop controls remain visible. It is not a learned-router result or a model-quality claim.

<div class="widget-panel" data-widget="token_level_recurrence" data-theorem-id="AIM-T0035" data-dictionary-ids="COMMON-0052 COMMON-0053 COMMON-0059 COMMON-0068 COMMON-0069"></div>

`AIM-B0012` turns learned token-level routing into auditable bookkeeping:

```text
training tokens
  -> learned phase-to-budget table
  -> held-out token budgets
  -> fixed, wrong-period, shifted-budget, and over-loop controls
  -> nonperiodic scalar-threshold control
```

This is useful only as a learned-router harness. It is not evidence that token-level recursive transformers improve a real model.

The widget below exposes the deterministic `AIM-B0012` fixture: a phase-to-budget lookup is fit on training tokens, applied to held-out tokens, and compared with wrong-period, shifted-budget, fixed-budget, over-loop, and nonperiodic scalar controls.

<div class="widget-panel" data-widget="learned_token_recurrence" data-dictionary-ids="COMMON-0052 COMMON-0053 COMMON-0059 COMMON-0068 COMMON-0069"></div>

`AIM-B0006` turns the training-free wrapper idea into an auditable fixture:

```text
sample
  -> circular phase budget
  -> run this many loop passes
  -> compare fixed, wrong-period, and over-loop controls
  -> compare nonperiodic scalar-threshold baseline
```

This is useful only as a schedule interface. It is not a claim that a reused block improves a real model.

The widget below exposes the deterministic `AIM-B0006` fixture. It compares a fixed phase-derived loop budget against single-pass, fixed-budget, wrong-period, over-loop, and nonperiodic scalar-threshold controls.

<div class="widget-panel" data-widget="training_free_loop_wrapper" data-dictionary-ids="COMMON-0052 COMMON-0053 COMMON-0054 COMMON-0067"></div>

`AIM-B0007` turns middle-block recurrence into auditable bookkeeping:

```text
selected loop block
  -> required block and budget
  -> full/fixed/wrong/over-loop controls
  -> block-pass accounting
```

Lean now pins down the contiguous selected-block route used by this fixture: the route stays inside the selected block range, closes after one selected-width shift, closes after any whole number of selected-width shifts, and selects the range start at sample zero. The combined route/budget facts additionally prove that the selected block and recurrence budget stay bounded together, initialize at `(start, 1)` for sample zero, and close after one product common cycle `width * loopPeriod` or any whole number of such cycles.

This is useful only as a schedule interface. It is not a claim that looping a middle block improves a real model.

`AIM-B0013` turns learned middle-block recurrence into auditable bookkeeping:

```text
training samples
  -> learned phase-to-block table
  -> learned phase-to-budget table
  -> held-out block and budget route
  -> selected-band, full-block, fixed-budget, wrong-period, wrong-block, and over-loop controls
```

This is useful only as a learned-router harness. It is not evidence that a learned middle-block recursive transformer improves quality, speed, memory, context length, perplexity, or reasoning.

The widget below exposes the deterministic `AIM-B0013` fixture. It fits one phase table for required model block and another phase table for loop budget, then compares the held-out route with selected-band, full-block, fixed-budget, wrong-period, wrong-block, and over-loop controls.

<div class="widget-panel" data-widget="learned_middle_block_recurrence" data-theorem-id="AIM-T0039" data-dictionary-ids="COMMON-0052 COMMON-0053 COMMON-0059 COMMON-0068 COMMON-0070"></div>

`AIM-B0008` turns multi-resolution recurrence into auditable bookkeeping:

```text
sample
  -> required loop budget
  -> required coarse/fine resolution
  -> single-resolution and wrong-resolution controls
  -> active-sample accounting
```

This is useful only as a schedule interface. It is not a claim that alternating compressed and full-resolution passes improves a real model.

`AIM-B0014` turns learned multi-resolution recurrence into auditable bookkeeping:

```text
training sample
  -> learned phase-to-budget table
  -> learned phase-to-resolution table
  -> held-out budget and coarse/fine route
  -> single-resolution, fixed-budget, wrong-period, and over-loop controls
```

This is useful only as a learned-router harness. It is not evidence that a learned compressed/full-resolution recursive transformer improves quality, speed, memory, context length, perplexity, or reasoning.

The widget below exposes the deterministic `AIM-B0014` fixture. It fits phase-to-budget and phase-to-resolution tables, then compares the held-out route with single-resolution, fixed-budget, wrong-period, and over-loop controls.

<div class="widget-panel" data-widget="learned_multi_resolution_recurrence" data-dictionary-ids="COMMON-0052 COMMON-0053 COMMON-0059 COMMON-0069 COMMON-0070"></div>

`AIM-B0009` turns learned recurrence schedules into auditable bookkeeping:

```text
training sample
  -> phase-to-budget lookup
  -> predicted loop budget
  -> fixed-budget, wrong-period, and over-loop controls
  -> schedule accuracy
```

This is useful only as a learned-schedule harness. It is not evidence that a learned recursive transformer improves quality, speed, memory, context length, perplexity, or reasoning.

The widget below exposes the deterministic `AIM-B0009` fixture. It is the baseline learned schedule case: fit a phase-to-budget table, then compare held-out budgets with fixed-budget, wrong-period, and over-loop controls.

<div class="widget-panel" data-widget="learned_recurrence_schedule" data-dictionary-ids="COMMON-0052 COMMON-0053 COMMON-0070"></div>

`AIM-B0015` adds the first tiny looped recurrent-state prototype:

```text
sample
  -> certified token recurrence budget
  -> finite hidden state after that many loop passes
  -> state-to-label lookup
  -> one-step, wrong-period, scalar, and nonperiodic controls
```

The Lean facts `AIM-T0049` through `AIM-T0055` certify only the finite state arithmetic: the state is bounded, one loop starts at zero, the certified budget recovers the sample phase, whole-period token shifts do not change the certified state read, and positive raw budgets close after one or many whole recurrent periods. This is useful as a prototype harness boundary. It is not evidence that a learned recursive transformer improves quality, speed, memory, context length, perplexity, or reasoning.

## Learning Path

1. Start with phase channels: finite indices, closure, and known-period examples.
2. Compare learned-feature baselines: cyclic features, dense scalar thresholds, learned absolute positions, and wrong-period controls.
3. Compare harmonic/Fourier features: correct sine/cosine phase features, wrong-frequency controls, and scalar controls.
4. Check backend parity: CPU references first, optional MLX only when available.
5. Add cyclic memory: slots, alias diagnostics, and nonperiodic controls.
6. Study coil retrieval: selected long-lag paths, local baselines, wrong-stride baselines, and full-attention oracle behavior.
7. Add content-gated retrieval: coil/local routing, wrong-gate controls, union/full-attention baselines, and candidate-budget accounting.
8. Add learned content-gate retrieval: phase-to-route lookup tables, route accuracy, wrong-period controls, flipped-gate controls, union/full-attention baselines, and candidate-budget accounting.
9. Add looped recurrence: loop phase, exit certificates, score traces, over-loop controls, and recursive transformer baselines.
10. Add token-level recurrence routing: per-token budgets, active-token counts, selected middle block, resolution labels, and wrong-loop controls.
11. Add learned token-level recurrence routing: phase-to-budget lookup tables, held-out token budgets, wrong-period controls, shifted-budget controls, over-loop controls, and scalar-threshold controls.
12. Add training-free loop wrappers: circular phase budgets, wrong-period controls, over-loop controls, scalar-threshold controls, and optional MLX scoring.
13. Add middle-block recurrence controls: selected-block schedules, full-block baselines, fixed budgets, wrong-block controls, over-loop controls, and block-pass accounting.
14. Add learned middle-block recurrence controls: phase-to-block lookup tables, phase-to-budget lookup tables, selected-band and full-block baselines, fixed budgets, wrong-period controls, wrong-block controls, over-loop controls, and block-pass accounting.
15. Add multi-resolution recurrence controls: coarse/fine phase routing, single-resolution baselines, fixed budgets, wrong-resolution controls, over-loop controls, and active-sample accounting.
16. Add learned multi-resolution recurrence controls: phase-to-budget lookup tables, phase-to-resolution lookup tables, single-resolution baselines, fixed budgets, wrong-period controls, over-loop controls, and active-sample accounting.
17. Add learned recurrence-schedule controls: phase-to-budget lookup tables, fixed-budget baselines, wrong-period controls, over-loop controls, and schedule accuracy.
18. Add tiny looped recurrent-state prototypes: certified budget state reads, state-to-label lookup, one-step controls, wrong-period controls, scalar controls, and nonperiodic controls.
19. Move to adapters, circulant mixers, and block-cyclic mixers: block sharing, circular-convolution parity, row/column residue cells, parameter accounting, alias diagnostics, and wrong-structure controls.
20. Add MultiCoil positions: combined periods, product-cycle/lcm closure, RoPE-style phase structure, and mismatch controls.
21. Compare RoPE-style relative phase: query/key lag labels, wrong-period controls, query-only controls, and scalar controls.
22. Add winding-aware positions: residue, winding, alias diagnostics, wrong-period controls, absolute-position controls, and scalar controls.
23. Only then compare learned model baselines and MLX prototypes, with quality, runtime, memory, parameter count, and interpretability reported separately.
24. Treat quaternion, spherical, Hopf/fiber, and `S^7` ideas as later geometry-aware AI lessons, not as shortcuts around the lower-dimensional evidence.

## What To Notice

- Phase-channel proofs are about finite indices, not model quality.
- `AIA-B0003` is backend parity scaffolding, not a speed, quality, or acceleration claim.
- `AIA-B0004` is a learned-feature baseline scaffold, not a neural-network result or real-workload benchmark.
- `AIA-B0005` is a harmonic/Fourier-feature scaffold, not a standard RoPE or language-model benchmark.
- Positive periodic fixtures must be paired with negative controls.
- `AIM-B0001` is a cyclic-memory benchmark harness, not a retrieval or attention theorem.
- `AIM-B0002` is a reachability harness over candidate index sets, not an attention-quality theorem.
- `AIM-B0004` is a gated-route reachability and budget harness, not a learned attention-quality theorem.
- `AIM-B0010` is learned route-table bookkeeping, not evidence about neural learned gates, retrieval quality, context length, runtime, or memory scaling.
- `AIM-B0016` is hybrid local+coil candidate-set reachability and budget bookkeeping, not evidence about sparse-attention quality, runtime, memory scaling, or long-context model behavior.
- `AIM-B0017` is finite stride-family candidate-set reachability and budget bookkeeping, not evidence about sparse-attention quality, multi-head attention quality, runtime, memory scaling, throughput, or long-context model behavior.
- `AIM-B0003` is a looped/recursive transformer schedule harness, not a recursive reasoning, context length, speed, memory, or model-quality result.
- `AIM-B0011` is loop-exit certificate bookkeeping, not evidence about adaptive-exit quality, reasoning, context length, speed, memory, or throughput.
- `AIM-B0005` is token-level recurrence routing bookkeeping, not evidence about learned routers, reasoning, perplexity, throughput, memory, or context length.
- `AIM-B0012` is learned token-level recurrence bookkeeping, not evidence about learned neural token routers, reasoning, perplexity, throughput, memory, or context length.
- `AIM-B0006` is training-free loop-wrapper bookkeeping, not evidence about learned recurrence, reasoning, language-model quality, throughput, memory, or context length.
- `AIM-B0007` is middle-block recurrence bookkeeping, not evidence about neural learned block routers, reasoning, perplexity, throughput, memory, or context length.
- `AIM-B0013` is learned middle-block recurrence bookkeeping, not evidence about neural block routers, reasoning, perplexity, throughput, memory, or context length.
- `AIM-B0008` is multi-resolution recurrence bookkeeping, not evidence about learned compressed/full-resolution routing, reasoning, perplexity, throughput, memory, or context length.
- `AIM-B0014` is learned multi-resolution recurrence bookkeeping, not evidence about neural compressed/full-resolution routing, reasoning, perplexity, throughput, memory, or context length.
- `AIM-B0009` is learned recurrence-schedule bookkeeping, not evidence about learned recursive transformer quality, reasoning, perplexity, throughput, memory, or context length.
- `AIM-B0015` is a tiny looped recurrent-state fixture, not evidence about learned recursive transformer quality, reasoning, perplexity, throughput, memory, or context length.
- `AIRA-B0001` is an adapter-block benchmark harness, not a CoilRA, RoPE, parameter-efficiency, or quality theorem.
- `AIRA-B0002` is a MultiCoil/RoPE-style positional harness, not a standard-RoPE comparison or language-model theorem.
- `AIRA-B0008` is two-period MultiCoil product-cycle closure bookkeeping, not evidence that the product horizon is minimal or useful for a learned positional encoding.
- `AIRA-B0003` is a RoPE-style relative phase harness, not an attention-quality, context-length, or language-model theorem.
- `AIRA-B0004` is parameter-count accounting, not evidence about quality, runtime, memory, training stability, or fine-tuning behavior.
- `AIRA-B0005` is circulant-mixer validation and parameter accounting, not evidence about neural-layer quality, runtime, memory, training stability, or hardware efficiency.
- `AIRA-B0006` is residue-plus-winding alias-control bookkeeping, not evidence about RoPE quality, context length, perplexity, runtime, or learned positional encodings.
- `AIRA-B0007` is block-cyclic dense-parity and alias-load bookkeeping, not evidence about neural-layer quality, parameter efficiency, runtime, memory, training stability, or hardware efficiency.
- MLX/Mac-first experiments should be reported as benchmarks, not proofs.

::: {.checkpoint-box}

## Checkpoint

Why is the nonperiodic control in `AIA-B0002` important before making any AI-improvement claim?

:::

## Source Trail

<p data-paper-ref="papers/applications/PAPER_AI_01_CIRCLE_AI_ARCHITECTURES.md">AI architecture paper: <a href="https://github.com/corbensorenson/circle-calculus/blob/main/papers/applications/PAPER_AI_01_CIRCLE_AI_ARCHITECTURES.md">Circle AI Architectures</a></p>
<p data-paper-ref="papers/applications/PAPER_AI_02_COIL_ATTENTION_AND_MEMORY.md">Memory paper: <a href="https://github.com/corbensorenson/circle-calculus/blob/main/papers/applications/PAPER_AI_02_COIL_ATTENTION_AND_MEMORY.md">Coil Attention And Memory</a></p>
<p data-paper-ref="papers/applications/PAPER_AI_03_COILRA_AND_MULTICOIL_ROPE.md">Adapter paper: <a href="https://github.com/corbensorenson/circle-calculus/blob/main/papers/applications/PAPER_AI_03_COILRA_AND_MULTICOIL_ROPE.md">CoilRA And MultiCoil RoPE</a></p>

::: {.warning-box}
Python and optional MLX benchmark fixtures are executable references only. They do not replace Lean proofs and do not prove that circles improve neural networks.
:::
